coredata.dc:title: Reaction-based colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of silver ions
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Hao-Yang
Gao, Ying
Li, Bin
Li, Chang-Wei
Guo, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: A novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe CHa based on Ag+-induced hydrolysis of hydrazone was designed and synthesized. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time the ratiometric sensing of Ag+ based on a chemical reaction has been accomplished. When Ag+ was added to a solution of CHa, a visible color change from yellow to colorless and a fluorescence color change from yellow to blue were observed. Probe CHa displayed a 203-fold enhancement of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I450nm/I520nm) toward Ag+ with outstanding sensitivity, high selectivity and a low detection limit (LOD 61 nM). Moreover, CHa was successfully applied to the ratiometric imaging of Ag+ in HepG2 and HeLa cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence probe
Ag+ detection
Reaction-based system
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21472148 and 21072158 ), Overseas Students Science and Technology Activities Project Merit Funding of Shaanxi Province (No. 20151190 ), the Special Foundation of the Education Committee of Shaanxi Province (No. 15JK1674 ), Academic Backbone of Northwest University Outstanding Youth Support Program and Top-rated Discipline construction scheme of Shaanxi higher education.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of CHa, CHb and CHc.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of probe CHa (10 μM) in the presence of different amounts of Ag+ (0 30 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of probe CHb (10 μM) in the presence of different amounts of Ag+ (0 30 μM) in EtOH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of probe CHc (10 μM) in the presence of different amounts of Ag+ (0 30 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of probe CHa (10 μM) upon addition of Ag+ (0 30 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C, λex = 400 nm, slit: 5 nm/5 nm; (b) Fluorescent spectra of probe CHb (10 μM) upon addition of Ag+ (0 30 μM) in EtOH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C, λex = 400 nm, slit: 5 nm/5 nm; (c) Fluorescent spectra of CHc (10 μM) upon addition of Ag+ (0 30 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C, λex = 430 nm, slit: 5 nm/5 nm.

(a) Linear plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I450nm/I520nm) of CHa (10 μM) against Ag+ concentrations (0 6 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (b) Linear plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I465nm/I520nm) of CHb (10 μM) against Ag+ concentrations (0 30 μM) in EtOH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (c) Fluorescence intensity (I527nm) of CHc (10 μM) as a function of Ag+ concentrations (0 10 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of probe CHa (10 μM) in the presence of various ions (100 μM for Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn2+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Na+ and Ba2+) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (b) Fluorescence spectra of probe CHb (10 μM) in the presence of various ions (100 μM for Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn2+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Na+ and Ba2+) in EtOH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (c) Fluorescence spectra of probe CHc in the presence of various ions (100 μM for Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn2+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Na+ and Ba2+) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C.

(a) Fluorescence responses of probe CHa (10 μM) toward Ag+ in the presence of various ions in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (b) Fluorescence responses of probe CHb (10 μM) toward Ag+ in the presence of various ions in EtOH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (c) Fluorescence responses of probe CHc (10 μM) toward Ag+ in the presence of various ions in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C. Orange bars represent the addition of a single species including: 1. Ag+, 2. Zn2+, 3. Cd2+, 4. Hg2+, 5. Fe3+, 6. Fe2+, 7. Co2+, 8. Ni2+, 9. Sn2+, 10. Pb2+, 11. Mg2+, 12. Cr3+, 13. Al3+, 14. Mn2+, 15. K+, 16. Ca2+, 17. Cu2+, 18. Na+, 19. Ba2+ (1–19: 100 μM). Blue bars represent the presence of Ag+ (30 μM) and other species. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of CHa (10 μM) in the absence and presence of Ag+ (30 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (b) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of CHb (10 μM) in the absence and presence of Ag+ (30 μM) in EtOH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (c) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of (c) CHc (10 μM) in the absence and presence of Ag+ (30 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C.

(a) Fluorescence spectra change of CHa (10 μM) treatment with Ag+ (30 μM) and then S2− (40 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (b) Fluorescence spectra change of CHb (10 μM) treatment with Ag+ (30 μM) and then S2− (40 μM) in EtOH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C; (c) Fluorescence spectra change of CHc (10 μM) treatment with Ag+ (30 μM) and then S2− (40 μM) in CH3OH/phosphate buffer (20.0 mM, pH = 7.4, 1:1, v/v) at 25 °C.
A proposed sensing mechanism of CHa for Ag+.

Partial 1H NMR spectra of CHa, the reaction product of CHa with Ag+ and compound 1a. The middle spectrum was obtained after simple purification of the reaction product of CHa with Ag+ by passage through a short silica column.

(a) First row: HepG2 cells were incubated with CHa (5 μM) for 30 min; second row: HepG2 cells were incubated with CHa (5 μM) for 30 min, followed by incubation of Ag+ (50 μM) for 2 h; (b) Relative ratio of blue/yellow fluorescence intensity of row 1, 2 in (a). The results are presented as means ± SE with replicates n = 3 (**p < 0.01). The ratio images were all obtained as Fblue/Fyellow. Images were acquired from 410 to 480 nm for blue fluorescence, and from 500 to 600 nm for yellow fluorescence. λex = 405 nm. Scale bar is 30 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) First row: HeLa cells were incubated with CHa (5 μM) for 30 min; second row: HeLa cells were incubated with CHa (5 μM) for 30 min, followed by incubation of Ag+ (50 μM) for 2 h; (b) Relative ratio of blue/yellow fluorescence intensity of row 1, 2 in (a). The results are presented as means ± SE with replicates n = 3 (**p < 0.01). The ratio images were all obtained as Fblue/Fyellow. Images were acquired from 410 to 480 nm for blue fluorescence, and from 500 to 600 nm for yellow fluorescence. λex = 405 nm. Scale bar is 30 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Reaction-based colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of silver ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Automation, Xi’an University of Posts and Telecommunications, Xi’an, 710121, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xi’an University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710127, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of the Ministry of Education
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Resource Biology and Biotechnology in Western China of the Ministry of Education, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Resource Biology and Biotechnology in Western China of the Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe CHa based on Ag+-induced hydrolysis of hydrazone was designed and synthesized. Upon addition of Ag+ to a solution of CHa, a visible color change from yellow to colorless and a fluorescence color change from yellow to blue were observed. The probe displayed a 203-fold enhancement of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I450nm/I520nm) toward Ag+ with outstanding sensitivity, high selectivity and a low detection limit. Furthermore, CHa was successfully applied to the ratiometric imaging of Ag+ in HepG2 and HeLa cells.

A novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe CHa based on Ag+-induced hydrolysis of hydrazone was designed and synthesized. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time the ratiometric sensing of Ag+ based on a chemical reaction has been accomplished. When Ag+ was added to a solution of CHa, a visible color change from yellow to colorless and a fluorescence color change from yellow to blue were observed. Probe CHa displayed a 203-fold enhancement of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I450nm/I520nm) toward Ag+ with outstanding sensitivity, high selectivity and a low detection limit (LOD 61 nM). Moreover, CHa was successfully applied to the ratiometric imaging of Ag+ in HepG2 and HeLa cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver (Ag) and silver-containing compounds are widely used in the photographic, electronic, pharmaceutical and imaging industries because of their unique physical and chemical properties [1–4]. Thus, along with the rapid development of industry and the economy, more and more Ag+ are discharged into the ecosystem, polluting water sources and affecting food and agriculture [5–8]. It is a well known fact that long-term exposure to excess silver ions can cause silver poisoning, heart enlargement, skin damage, growth retardation and other diseases in humans [9]. However, silver ions can also have a beneficial bactericidal effect that has long been used by humans [10–14]. In view of the dual role of silver ions, it is necessary to develop more selective and sensitive detection methods to trace the content of Ag+ in the environment and to study the physiological functions of Ag+.

In recent years, ion selective electrodes, electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry, microextraction, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and other instrumental methods have been widely applied to detect Ag+ [15–19]. Compared with conventional methods, fluorescent probes have attracted more attention for their simplicity, low cost, good selectivity, real-time detection and nondestructive testing. Hitherto, a variety of fluorescent probes for the detection of Ag+ have been developed, and their sensing mechanisms are mainly based on chemical reaction [20–26], complexation reaction [27–32], quantum dots [33,34], nanoparticles [35,36] and other strategies [37]. Among these methods, reaction-based fluorescence probes have become more popular in research as the irreversible chemical reaction between the probe and the analyte can make the probe more precise and sensitive. In recent years, steady progress has been made in the study of reaction type probes for Ag+ (Table S1). Most of the reaction-based Ag+-selective fluorescent probes are not only easily interfered with by other heavy metal ions such as Hg2+ and Cu2+ but also influenced by many factors including photobleaching, probe concentration and test conditions. Conversely, ratiometric fluorescence probes can avoid outside interference through the self-calibration of two emission bands. It would therefore be extremely useful to develop a highly selective reaction-based fluorescence probe that can achieve ratiometric detection for Ag+.

In the present study, we developed for the first time a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence probe CHa based on Ag+-induced hydrolysis of the hydrazone derivative of a coumarin fluorophore. It displays a long-wavelength yellow emission due to the ICT effect. The addition of Ag+ can promote the hydrolysis of CHa to release 3-formyl substituted coumarin derivatives that exhibit short-wavelength blue emission. This enables the probe to perform the ratiometric and colorimetric detection of Ag+ with good sensitivity, prominent selectivity and a low fluorescence detection limit. CHa shows low cytotoxicity and it can be applied for the ratiometric imaging of Ag+ in HepG2 and HeLa cells. It is a suitable research tool to study the role of Ag+ in a biological environment.

Unless otherwise specified, all reagents and solvents were purchased from commercial suppliers and were used without further treatment. NMR spectra of all compounds were obtained on a Varian Unity INOVA-400 spectrometer (400 MHz for 1H NMR and 100 MHz for 13C NMR). High-resolution mass spectra (HRMS) of all compounds were measured using a Bruker micrOTOF-Q II mass spectrometer. IR spectra were obtained on a Bruker TENSOR27 FT-IR spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were determined with a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-1700 spectrometer. All pH measurements were achieved with a Model PHS-3C meter. Cell imaging experiments were performed under a confocal laser scanning microscope (Olympus FV 1000) with excitation at 405 nm. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction data were collected on a Bruker SMART APEXCCD X-ray diffractometer.

Compounds 1a, 1b, 1c and 2-hydrazinobenzothiazole were prepared according to previous reports (Scheme S1-S4). The synthesis routes of CHa, CHb and CHc are shown in Scheme 1 . The characterization of all compounds is given in Fig. S10-S27.

Compound 1a (0.190 g, 1.0 mmol) and 2-hydrazinobenzothiazole (0.165 g, 1.0 mmol) were dissolved in anhydrous ethanol (50.0 mL). The reaction mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 20 h. Then the yellow precipitate formed was filtered and washed three times with cold ethanol to afford the desired product (0.284 g, yield: 84.3%). m.p. 250–252 °C, 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d 6) δ: 10.85 (s, 1H), 8.35 (s, 1H), 8.17 (s, 1H), 7.81 (d, J = 4.0 Hz, 1H), 7.77 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 1H), 7.49 (s, 1H), 7.34 (t, J = 7.0 Hz, 1H), 7.15 (t, J = 7.0 Hz,1H), 6.87 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 1 Hz), 6.79 (s, 1H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO-d 6) δ: 167.28, 162.46, 160.55, 155.72, 138.39, 137.64, 131.32, 129.91, 129.80, 126.47, 122.24, 121.98, 118.49, 116.95, 114.33, 112.07, 102.57. HRMS calcd for C17H12N3O3S [M + H]+ 338.0594, found 338.0578.

Compound 1b (0.204 g, 1.0 mmol) and 2-hydrazinobenzothiazole (0.165 g, 1.0 mmol) were dissolved in anhydrous ethanol (50.0 mL). The reaction mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 20 h. Then the yellow precipitate formed was filtered and washed three times with cold ethanol to afford the desired product (0.215 g, yield: 61.3%). m.p. 232–233 °C, 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d 6) δ: 10.70 (s, 1H), 8.06 (s, 1H), 7.64 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 2H), 7.30 (d, J = 6.0 Hz, 1H), 7.23 (m, 1H), 7.04 (m, 1H), 6.79 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 1H), 6.72 (s, 1H), 2.25 (s, 3H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO-d 6) δ:173.10, 166.98, 164.80, 160.65, 146.64, 141.30, 141.09, 135.78, 134.84, 131.21, 127.05, 126.78, 119.78, 118.84, 116.60, 111.57, 107.01, 21.80. HRMS calcd for C18H14N3O3S [M + H]+ 352.0720, found 352.0714.

Compound 1c (0.259 g, 1.0 mmol) and 2-hydrazinobenzothiazole (0.165 g, 1.0 mmol) were dissolved in anhydrous ethanol (50.0 mL). The reaction mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 20 h. Then the yellow precipitate formed was filtered and washed three times with cold ethanol to afford the desired product (0.195 g, yield: 48.0%). m.p. 194–196 °C, 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ: 11.63 (s, 1H), 8.02 (s, 1H), 7.70 (m, 1H), 7.57 (d, J = 8.88 Hz, 1H), 7.27 (m, 1H), 7.07 (s, 1H), 6.73 (d, J = 8.08 Hz, 1H), 6.56 (s, 1H), 3.46 (m, 4H), 2.29 (s, 3H), 1.13 (t, J = 6.2 Hz, 6H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3) δ: 168.15, 161.18, 156.94, 151.16, 150.76, 147.52, 142.07, 130.56, 129.97, 126.05, 122.25, 121.24, 119.22, 118.27, 118.84, 109.25, 108.57, 96.86, 44.94, 15.43, 12.49. HRMS calcd for C22H23N4O2S [M + H]+ 407.1536, found 407.1547.

Various stock solutions (10.0 mM) of the nitrate salts of Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn2+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Na+ and Ba2+ were prepared in twice distilled water. Stock solutions of the CHa and CHc probes (1 mM) were prepared in CH3OH. Stock solutions of probe CHb (1 mM) were prepared in EtOH. For all measurements, fluorescence spectra were obtained by excitation at 400 nm (CHa, CHb) or 430 nm (CHc). Both the excitation slit and the emission slit were 5 nm.

HepG2 and HeLa cells were grown in Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 (RPMI 1640) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% penicillin and streptomycin in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air at 37 °C in the incubator. The CCK-8 cell proliferation assays were applied to determine the viabilities of the cells treated with CHa. Cells were prepared in 96-well plates (1×104 cells per well) for 24 h. After cell attachment, different concentrations of CHa (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 μM) were added to the 96-well plates. After 24 h of incubation, cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and stained with CCK8. Four hours later, the cell viabilities were measured at 450 nm on a microplate spectrometer.

For confocal fluorescence imaging, HepG2 and HeLa cells were seeded into 35 mm glass bottom cell culture dishes (φ20 mm, NEST Biotechnology) for 24 h. For the control experiment, cells were incubated with 5 μM of probe CHa for 30 min, then washed with PBS (pH 7.4) three times. For the detection of Ag+, cells were incubated with 5 μM of probe CHa for 30 min, then treated with 50 μM of Ag+ for another 2 h. Cell imaging was performed after the cells had been washed with PBS three times.

As shown in Scheme 1, CHa, CHb and CHc were conveniently synthesized via the condensation of 2-hydrazinobenzothiazole with 7-hydroxy-3-formyl coumarin (1a), 7-hydroxyl-3-acetyl coumarin (1b) and 7-(diethylamine)-3-acetyl coumarin (1c) respectively. By 1H NMR characterization and X-ray single crystal data analysis (Fig. S22, Fig. S28 and Table S2-S5), it was found that the structure of CHc is different from that of CHa and CHb. The proposed reaction mechanism for the formation of CHc is shown in Fig. 1 . Compound 1c reacts with 2-hydrazinobenzothiazole to form the hydrazone intermediate 2 via a simple condensation reaction. Compared with the structures of CHa and CHb, the strong donor ability of the substituent at the 7-position of hydrazone intermediate 2 enhances the nucleophilicity of secondary nitrogen atoms (-NH-). In addition, the large steric hindrance of the methyl substituent makes secondary nitrogen atoms approach the carbonyl of the coumarin lactone ring, which facilitates the occurrence of the intramolecular nucleophilic substitution reaction. For these reasons, the hydrazone intermediate 2 engages in a further aminolysis reaction, destroying the lactone structure and turning it into CHc.

The influence of pH on the fluorescence response of probes CHa, CHb and CHc was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, in the absence of Ag+, the fluorescence intensity of the probe CHa showed no obvious change in the pH range of 5.5–10.5. Despite its similar structure characteristics to CHa, CHb was found to be sensitive to change in the pH value, which is most probably because the extra methyl of CHb makes the secondary nitrogen atom attack the carbonyl of the lactone moiety more readily, triggering intramolecular ester ammonolysis. From the infrared spectrometric analysis, CHb shows a band assigned to the carbonyl stretching vibration for the lactone moiety at 1693 cm−1 (Fig. S20). In contrast, the corresponding compound CHb-1 obtained at high pH (9–12) does not show this band but shows a new band at 1638 cm−1 assigned to amide carbonyl (Fig. S9), which reveals that intramolecular ester ammonolysis of the CHb lactone moiety may take place (Fig. S7). After treatment with Ag+, the excellent fluorescence response of the probes to Ag+ was observed in the pH range of 7.0–9.0, indicating that the three probes can play a good role in physiological conditions. UV–vis titration experiments of the probes toward Ag+ were carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 a, probe CHa exhibited a maximum absorption peak at 445 nm in the absence of Ag+. Nevertheless, the addition of an increasing concentration of Ag+ caused a distinct decrease in the absorption peak at 445 nm and a gradual increase at 345 nm. For probe CHb, the original absorption peak at 475 nm gradually decreased and an absorption peak appeared at 330 nm upon the addition of Ag+ to the solution of CHb (Fig. 2b), suggesting that probe CHb can respond to Ag+. Fig. 2c shows that two absorption bands of CHc at 430 nm and 490 nm gradually decreased after the addition of Ag+. Moreover, all probe solutions showed a clearly visible color change from yellow to colorless (Fig. 2, inset), which suggested that the three probes could be suitable for detecting Ag+ with the naked eye.

To investigate the fluorescence intensity changes with an increasing amount of Ag+, the fluorescence titration experiments of the probes were evaluated. Interestingly, both CHa and CHb showed ratiometric fluorescent responses toward Ag+, but CHc showed fluorescent quenching and a blue shift of the fluorescent emission wavelength after the treatment with Ag+. As shown in Fig. 3 a, upon the addition of increasing amounts of Ag+, the fluorescence intensity of CHa at 520 nm decreased and a new emission peak at 450 nm increased. In addition, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm and 520 nm changed from 0.038 to 7.710 (Fig. 3a, inset) corresponding to a 203-fold enhancement, which demonstrated that probe CHa can be used to detect Ag+ in a ratiometric manner. Probe CHb showed two main emission bands at 465 nm and 520 nm. However, increasing the concentration of Ag+ induced a slight increase in the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm and a noticeable decrease at 520 nm (Fig. 3b). Meanwhile, the strong background fluorescence of probe CHb at a short wavelength (465 nm) led to a slight change in the fluorescence intensity ratio from 0.777 to 3.748 (4.82-fold enhancement), which was less favorable for detecting Ag+ compared to CHa. Then, we studied the emission spectrum of CHc (Fig. 3c). Unlike probes CHa and CHb, the fluorescence intensity of probe CHc decreased at 527 nm and a blue shift occurred in the fluorescent emission wavelength of probe CHc with the addition of increasing amounts of Ag+, which might be attributable to the molecular structure of probe CHc which is different from that of probe CHa and CHb. Among the three probes, CHa is the best suited for the detection of Ag+.

A good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratios (CHa or CHb) or fluorescence intensity (CHc) and the concentration of Ag+ was obtained (Fig. 4 ). The probes can therefore be classified as chemical dosimeters. The detection limits (DL) of CHa, CHb and CHc for Ag+ respectively are 61 nM, 6.8 μM and 2.4 μM calculated according to the following formula [38]: DL = 3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurement and K is the slope of the calibration curve. Compared with CHb and CHc, CHa has the lowest detection limit of Ag+, which indicates that it is the most sensitive to Ag+.

To further demonstrate the selectivity of the probes toward different analytes, the fluorescence spectra of the probes in the presence of various metal cations (Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mn2+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Na+ and Ba2+) were investigated, as shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen in Fig. 5a, the addition of Ag+ to the solution of CHa resulted in an obvious decrease in emission intensity at 520 nm and a non-negligible increase at 450 nm, causing a ratiometric response to Ag+. Under the same test conditions, other metal ions did not interfere with the detection of Ag+, among which Co2+, Ni2+, Fe3+ and Hg2+ only caused a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 520 nm. In Fig. 5b, it can be seen that none of the metal ions except Ag+ elicited an increase at 465 nm, although the addition of Cu2+ and Hg2+ also produced a distinct decrease in emission intensity at 520 nm. Then the selectivity of probe CHc was studied (Fig. 5c). As shown in the figure, the fluorescence of probe CHc can be quenched by Ag+, Ni2+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, which indicates that probe CHc is unsuitable for the specific detection of Ag+. Moreover, the competitive experiments confirmed that only the probe CHa possessed high selectivity for Ag+ over other metal ions and especially was nearly inert to competing metal ions such as Cu2+ and Hg2+ (Fig. 6 ). There may be two factors contributing to this. Firstly, the probe CHa containing sulfur atom has a high affinity for soft transition metal ions including Ag+ [39]. The second factor is that Ag+ can induce hydrolysis of the hydrazone moiety in the probe. We also studied the UV–vis responses of CHa, CHb and CHc to various possible interfering metal ions. From the absorption study, the selectivity of the probes towards Ag+ was not affected by the presence of the other tested ions, including Hg2+ and Cu2+ (Fig. S8).

The time dependent fluorescence responses of the probes toward Ag+ were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 7 , a plateau of fluorescence changes was reached after 120 min, 250 min and 4 min respectively for CHa, CHb and CHc, hinting that the response mechanism of probe CHc is very different from that of CHa and CHb. In order to further investigate the binding of probes with Ag+, the Job’s plots were tested by using a total concentration of probe and Ag+ i.e. 20 μM. The results indicated that the combination of probe (CHa, CHb or CHc) and Ag+ was 1:1 stoichiometry (Fig. S2).

The reversibility of the Ag+ sensing process was investigated by using the chelation of S2− with Ag+. As shown in Fig. 8 , upon the addition of S2− there were no obvious fluorescence intensity changes in the CHa or CHb pre-treated solution (10 μM probe with 30 μM Ag+), thus indicating that the sensing process was possibly based on an irreversible chemical reaction. However, upon the addition of Ag+ to the CHc pre-treated solution (10 μM probe with 30 μM Ag+), the fluorescence intensity was restored to the same intensity as that of probe CHc, thus showing that the sensing process of CHc was reversible and could be based on a complex reaction.

The possible sensing mechanism of CHa is shown in Fig. 9 . CHa exhibited long-wavelength yellow emission, which was ascribed to the typical ICT (intramolecular charge transfer) effect. After adding Ag+, probe CHa first formed a 1:1 complex with Ag+ and then fluorescence enhancement was observed at short-wavelength because Ag+ promoted the hydrolysis of hydrazone to release compound 1a having short-wavelength emission. To demonstrate the sensing mechanism of CHa for Ag+, the reaction product of CHa with Ag+ was obtained and purified. As shown in Fig. 10 , the 1H NMR spectrum of the purified reaction product was consistent with that of compound 1a. Then, the HRMS spectra of the purified reaction product were measured. The m/z = 191.0356 was attributed to [1a + H]+ (calcd 191.0339) (Fig. S3). These results showed that the reaction product was compound 1a, which was in accordance with the predicted sensing mechanism. The sensing mechanism of CHb was the same as CHa.

In contrast, probe CHc can bind with Ag+ due to its own special structure characteristics, which quenched the fluorescence of CHc and led to a blue shift in the fluorescent emission wavelength of CHc. The possible sensing mechanism of CHc is shown in Fig. S4. Fluorescence quenching and blue shift occur when probe CHc is combined with Ag+, which is attributed to the chelation enhanced fluorescence quenching (CHEQ) effect and a weakened ICT effect. To explore the sensing mechanism of CHc for Ag+, HRMS spectrometry analyses were conducted. The m/z = 513.0506 was assignable to the complex species [CHc + Ag]+ (calcd 513.0509) (Fig. S5).

In view of the excellent properties of probe CHa for sensing Ag+ in vitro, the ability of probe CHa for cell imaging was evaluated in HepG2 cells and HeLa cells. The cytotoxicity of probe CHa was firstly measured by a CCK-8 assay (Fig. S6). The results exhibited that the cell viability was over 80%, which suggested that CHa has low cytotoxicity. Subsequently, the fluorescence imaging of probe CHa in HepG2 cells and HeLa cells was carried out. As shown in Figs. 11 and 12
, after cells had been incubated with a 5.0 μM solution of CHa for 30 min, weak fluorescence signals in the blue channel (410–480 nm) and strong fluorescence signals in the yellow channel (500–600 nm) were observed, indicating that probe CHa had great cell permeability. Furthermore, when cells were pre-treated with CHa and further incubated with 50.0 μM Ag+ at 37 °C for 2 h, the fluorescence intensity in the blue channel increased, along with a significant decrease of fluorescence intensity in the yellow channel. These results demonstrated that CHa can be used for monitoring Ag+ in living cells.

In summary, based on the Ag+-induced hydrolysis reaction of the hydrazone, we have developed a novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe CHa for selectively detecting Ag+. The reaction-based ratiometric probe shows not only outstanding sensitivity and a low detection limit but also excellent selectivity over other competing ions such as Cu2+ and Hg2+ in a physiological pH aqueous solution. Moreover, the excellent sensing performance and biocompatibility of probe CHa in cells suggested that it can be successfully applied for the ratiometric detection of Ag+.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21472148 and 21072158), Overseas Students Science and Technology Activities Project Merit Funding of Shaanxi Province (No. 20151190), the Special Foundation of the Education Committee of Shaanxi Province (No. 15JK1674), Academic Backbone of Northwest University Outstanding Youth Support Program and Top-rated Discipline construction scheme of Shaanxi higher education.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.05.064.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor for silver ions
5,10,15-Tris(pentafluoro –phenyl)corrole as highly selective neutral carrier for a silver ion-sensitive electrode
Bioaccumulation and toxicity of silver compounds: a review
Toxicity of silver nanoparticles in zebrafish models
In vivo imaging of transport and biocompatibility of single silver nanoparticles in early development of zebrafish embryos
Silver nanoparticle impact on bacterial growth: effect of pH, concentration, and organic matter
Off-line determination of trace silver in water samples and standard reference materials by cloud point extractionatomic absorption spectrometry
Silver selective electrodes based on methyl-2-pyridyl ketone oxime, phenyl-2-pyridyl ketone oxime and bis[2-(o-carboxythiophenoxy)methyl]-4-bromo-1-methoxy –benzene carriers
Bactericidal actions of a silver ion solution on escherichia coli, studied by energy-filtering transmission electron microscopy and proteomic analysis
Silver (I)-imidazole cyclophane gem-diol complexes encapsulated by electrospun tecophilic nanofibers: formation of nanosilver particles and antimicrobial activity
Interaction of silver nitrate with readily identifiable groups: relationship to the antibacterial action of silver ions
Interaction of silver (I) ions with the respiratory chain of escherichia coli: An electrochemical and scanning electrochemical microscopy study of the antimicrobial mechanism of micromolar Ag+
A mechanistic study of the antibacterial effect of silver ions on escherichia coli and staphylococcus aureus
Silver ion-selective electrodes using π-coordinate calix[4] arene derivatives as soft neutral carriers
A highly sensitive electrochemical assay for silver ion detection based on un-labeled C-rich ssDNA probe and controlled assembly of MWCNTs
Novel potentiometric and optical silver ion-selective sensors with subnanomolar detection limits
Sensitive and selective detection of Ag+ in aqueous solutions using Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles as smart electrochemical nanosensors
Multimethod quantification of Ag+ release from nanosilver
Selective fluorogenic and chromogenic probe for detection of silver ions and silver nanoparticles in aqueous media
Fluorometric chemodosimetry. Mercury (II) and silver (I) indication in water via enhanced fluorescence signaling
Highly sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of Ag (I) ion using 3,3′,5,5′,-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as an indicator
A coumarin-based fluorescent probe for Hg2+ and Ag+ with an N’-acetylthioureido group as a fluorescence switch
Imaging different interactions of mercury and silver with live cells by a designed fluorescence probe rhodamine B selenolactone
A nanoparticle autocatalytic sensor for Ag+ and Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution with high sensitivity and selectivity and its application in test paper
Fluorescence sensing of Ag+ ions by desulfurization of an acetylthiourea derivative of 2-(2-hydroxyphenyl) benzothiazole
Azadithiacrown ether based ditopic receptors capable of simultaneous multi-ionic recognition of Ag+ and Hg2+
An intramolecular charge transfer fluorescent probe: synthesis and selective fluorescent sensing of Ag+
Aza-crown-ether-appended xanthene: selective ratiometric fluorescent probe for silver (I) ion based on arene?metal ion interaction
Naphthalene based lab-on-a-molecule for the highly selective and sensitive detection of CN- and Ag+ in aqueous solution
A colorimetric and reversible fluorescent chemosensor for Ag+ in aqueous solution and its application in IMPLICATION logic gate
A 2-Styryl-1,8-naphthyridine derivative as a versatile fluorescent probe for the selective recognition of Hg2+, Ag+ and F− ions by tuning the solvent
One pot synthesis of highly fluorescent N doped C-dots and used as fluorescent probe detection for Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution
Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensing probes for Ag+ ions detection
A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of silver ions using graphene quantum dots
Tricyclic dihydropyrimidine-based multifunctional organic nanoparticles for detection of Ag (I) ions and spermidine: real-time application
A BSA-squaraine hybrid system for selectively detecting Ag+ in absolute PBS and sequential construction of logic functions
A 4-hydroxynaphthalimide-derived ratiometric fluorescent chemodosimeter for imaging palladium in living cells
Design strategies for water-soluble small molecular chromogenic and fluorogenic probes

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Prof. Yuan Guo, born in 1979, China, obtained her B. S. in 2001 and Ph. D. in 2006 from Department of Chemistry, Northwest University, China. She spent two years in France, as Post-doctoral research associate (2012–2014) at Institut de Chimie Organique et Analytique, Université d'Orléans, UMR CNRS. She served as Associate Professor of Chemistry (2009–2015) and Full Professor of Chemistry (2015-present) at Northwest University. Research interests: Biomimetic organic synthesis; Probe design and Chemosensing; Metal-catalyzed coupling reaction and its application in synthesis of heterocyclic compounds.
